coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of porous graphitic carbon nitride modulated by up-conversion carbon quantum dots for visible light-triggered photocatalysis towards bacteria inactivation
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Na
Liu, Shiji
Xu, Qidi
Yuan, Pingchuan
Zhang, Ping
Zhuo, Shujuan
Zhu, Changqing
Du, Jinyan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel porous g-C3N4 nanosheets modulated by up-conversion carbon quantum dots (CQDs/CNS) were successfully fabricated. Using the photocatalytic inactivation of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) as the model reaction, the visible light-triggered antibacterial activity was evaluated. It was found that the photocatalytic performance towards bacterial inactivation is significantly improved, with the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) in CQDs/CNS against E. coli and S. aureus only being 12.5 and 40 ppm, respectively. The enhancement in antibacterial behavior can be attributed to the introduction of up-conversion CQDs. On the one hand, it extends absorption region to the visible light which promotes the generation of e- - h+ pairs. On the other hand, it narrows the energy band gap which accelerates the separation of carriers and transferance of charges at the interface. This study provides a new insight for developing a CQDs-based g-C3N4 system for effective bacterial disinfection.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of porous graphitic carbon nitride modulated by up-conversion carbon quantum dots for visible light-triggered photocatalysis towards bacteria inactivation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel porous g-C3N4 nanosheets modulated by up-conversion carbon quantum dots (CQDs/CNS) were successfully fabricated. Using the photocatalytic inactivation of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) as the model reaction, the visible light-triggered antibacterial activity was evaluated. It was found that the photocatalytic performance towards bacterial inactivation is significantly improved, with the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) in CQDs/CNS against E. coli and S. aureus only being 12.5 and 40 ppm, respectively. The enhancement in antibacterial behavior can be attributed to the introduction of up-conversion CQDs. On the one hand, it extends absorption region to the visible light which promotes the generation of e- - h+ pairs. On the other hand, it narrows the energy band gap which accelerates the separation of carriers and transferance of charges at the interface. This study provides a new insight for developing a CQDs-based g-C3N4 system for effective bacterial disinfection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The porous up-conversion CQDs modulated g-C3N4 nanosheets (CQDs/CNS) possess an excellent visible-light-driven bacteriostatic and antibacterial performance with a low MIC100% of 12.5 and 40 ppm against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, because of the extension of light absorption region and the narrowness of energy bandgap.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous development of human society is accompanied by the continuous evolution of biological species. In today's world, public health problems are still facing enormous challenges, especially the spread of 2019 pandemic coronavirus disease (COVID-19). The increased incidence rate of bacterial infection cannot be underestimated and seriously threatens human health. The traditional antibiotic treatment makes bacteria resistant to drugs, leading to an increase in incidence rate [1,2]. As the most common pathogens, E. coli and S. aureus are the representatives of the Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, respectively [3]. At present, these two types of bacteria are mainly limited to the use of sensitive antibiotics, which inevitably generates the drug-resistant strains [4]. Therefore, non-antibiotic treatment has become a breakthrough point that cannot be ignored in the medical community.

In recent years, various methods and techniques have been explored to address the issue of antimicrobial resistance. Amongst, photocatalytic technology has attracted great attention due to its controllability and safety. Common photocatalysts, e.g., TiO2 [5], ZnO [6], and MoS2 [7], however, have limited utilization in the visible range. Moreover, most of them are highly toxic [8]. Due to the suitable bandgap (≈ 2.7 eV), high efficiency, and low toxicity, graphite nitride carbon (g-C3N4) has received intensive interests in the last two decades [9,10]. However, the actual application is limited because of low utilization efficiency of sunlight and high recombination rate of photo-generated carriers [11]. Various methods, such as precious metal modification [12], non-metallic elements adulteration [13], and heterojunctions construction [14], etc, have been adopted to improving the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4.

The winning theme of the 2023 Nobel Prize in Chemistry is "Discovery and Synthesis of Quantum Dots (QDs)". As a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted intensive attention due to their non-toxicity, good water-solubility, excellent conductivity, as well as distinct quantum size effect [15]. In particular, some of CQDs have unique up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. Specifically, the CQDs can be excited by 500 - 900 nm long wavelength light then emit 400 - 550 nm short wavelength light, thereby promoting the absorption and utilization efficiency of sunlight [16]. The merits of CQDs have significantly widened their applications in many aspects, including imaging [17], sensing [18], energy storage [19], photodynamic antibacterial therapy[20], electrocatalysis and photocatalysis[21]. In particular, by combining with inorganic semiconductor materials, e.g., CQDs/Bi2MoO6 [22], CQDs/Fe2O3 [23], CQDs/TiO2 [24], CQDs/BiOCl [25], and CQDs/Bi2WO6 [26], the photo response was found to be enhanced with the suppress of the recombination of photo-generated carriers, thereby the photocatalytic activity of CQDs was greatly improved under the irradiation of visible light. Considering their separate advantages, we anticipated that the cooperation of CQDs and g-C3N4 may provide a new platform for effective bacterial disinfection.

Currently, CQDs/g-C3N4 composites have been widely used in the photocatalysis of hydrogen evolution [27], carbon dioxide reduction [28], antibiotic degradation [29], and biomedicine [30]. The mechanism of activity enhancement was usually ascribed to the synergistic effect of photosensitization, promoted charge separation, and up-conversion fluorescence effect of CQDs [31]. Interestingly, CQDs play various roles in photocatalytic systems, such as electronic media, electronic reservoirs, photosensitizers, spectral converters, and individual photocatalysts, etc. [32]. In addition, the unique PL characteristics of the up-conversion CQDs have been discovered, which can transfer low-energy photons to high-energy photons on the CQDs by sequentially absorbing two or multiple longer wavelength photons [15].

Despite a large number of investigations in CQDs-involved photocatalysis, so far, there have been few reports on the utilization of up-conversion CQD modified g-C3N4 nanosheet as photocatalysts for sterilization. Therefore, it has an important theoretical and practical significance to construct CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst in enhancing the photocatalytic performance driven by visible light and its application in inactivating bacteria. Herein, using a facile hydrothermal method, new porous up-conversion CQDs decorated g-C3N4 nanosheets (CQDs/CNS) were successfully fabricated and used as a visible light-triggered photocatalyst to disinfect bacteria. We first characterized the structure and morphology of CQDs/CNS, and then explored their optoelectronic properties and catalytic performance under the optimal operational parameters. Finally, we proposed a reasonable mechanism of activity enhancement through the capture experiments and the electron spin resonance analysis. This study offers a new platform on green catalysis that develops a CQDs-based g-C3N4 system for effective bacterial disinfection.

Melamine (C3H6N6), citric acid, urea, ascorbic acid, agar, yeast extract, peptone, NaCl, AgNO3, and NH4Cl were purchased from Aladdin Corporation (Shanghai, China). RPMI 1640 medium, fetal bovine serum, trypsin-EDTA solution and Penicillin-Streptomycin solution were purchased from the Gibco (USA). Human gastric cancer cells SGC-7901 were purchased from the Beyotime institute of biotechnology (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and utilized as-received without any further purification. Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were purchased from Shanghai Beinuo Biotechnology Co. LTD (strain number: ATCC25922 and ATCC25923, respectively).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on D8 Advance Xray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα X-ray source. The morphologies were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM 8100, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM HT-7700, Hitachi, Japan). The elemental mapping of the samples was evaluated via field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) fitted out with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (FESEM device, FEI Tecnai F20). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of the samples was measured on a Micro metrics ASAP 2460 device using N2 adsorption/desorption. ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) was tested on U-3900 H spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were conducted on F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Mott-Schottky plots and transient photocurrent were measured using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai, Chenhua, China). Electron spin resonance (Bruker EMXplus) was used to capture reactive oxygen species (ROS) production under illumination. Visible light was obtained from a 300 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300) with a 420 nm cut-off filter. The incubator (BSD-TX345) was required for the bacterial culture.

The CNS were synthesized by a simple method. Typically, 3 g of melamine and 6 g of ammonium chloride were dissolved in water, then transferred into a crucible and calcined at 550 °C for 2 h in a Muffle furnace with a ramp rate of 10 °C·min−1. The resulted pale-yellow powder (CNS) was milled into powder in an agate mortar for further use.

The CQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal methods. To be specific, 3 g of citric acid and 1 g of urea were dissolved in 20 mL of water and then transferred to 50 mL of Teflon reactor to react at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting dark green solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm to remove large particles and the supernatant was then dialyzed and freeze-dried to obtain CQDs sample.

The CQDs/CNS composites were prepared as follows: an appropriate amount of CQDs was added to a beaker containing 20 mL of ethanol and water, wherein 0.1 g CNS was dispersed. After mixed and stirred for 30 min, the mixture solution was then transferred to a Teflon reactor and heated at 180 °C for 24 h to obtain the CQDs/CNS photocatalyst. The X wt% CQDs/CNS samples with different CQDs mass were prepared through the same procedure (where X = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, representing the mass percentage of CQDs to CNS in the composites). The as-obtained light green samples were named dried at 60 °C for 12 h, ground for further use.

Electrochemical Mott-Schottky measurements were carried out at CHI660E electrochemical station with Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared by dispersing 5 mg photocatalyst into 1 mL polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, 5 wt%) aqueous solution and stirring for 2 h to form a uniform slurry. Then, the mixture of 7 μL is dropped on the glass carbon electrode and dried overnight at 60 °C. Electrochemical tests were performed in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution [33].

A typical three electrode system consisting of a working electrode (sample electrode), a counter electrode (platinum wire), and a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl electrode) was used to test the photoelectrochemical properties. The working electrode was prepared by dip-coating photocatalyst slurry on an indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass and drying before measurement. PBS (pH 7.0) was selected as the supporting electrolyte, and a 300 W Xenon lamp was used as a visible light source for photocurrent and impedance measurements. The photoelectric response measurement is carried out at 0.0 V bias. The EIS spectra were recorded in the range of 0.01 Hz to 100,000 Hz at an alternating current (AC) voltage of 10 mV.

In order to determine viable bacterial counts, we analyzed the relationship between the OD600 value and bacteria colony forming units (CFU) on LB agar [34]. A linear relationship between OD600 and CFU was observed for E. coli (Y = 61.061 x − 4.597) and S. aureus (Y = 65.259 x − 6.553) in Fig. S1.

E. coli and S. aureus were selected as experimental subjects to explore the photocatalytic activity. Briefly, 20 μL of E. coli (S.aureus) was transferred from the − 80 °C refrigerator to 20 mL of luria-Bertani (LB) liquid medium, and then cultured in incubator with a shaking speed of 180 rpm at 37 °C for 12 h to an optical density absorption value of 0.5 at 600 nm (OD600 = 0.5).

The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was determined by monitoring the growth curve of the bacteria. In detail, different volumes of photocatalytic materials were homogeneously mixed with the bacteria and diluted with liquid LB medium. Subsequently, a Xenon lamp (300 W) with a UV cutoff filter (λ < 420 nm) was used as the visible light source for 60 min. Finally, the mixture was placed in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h, the change of OD600 was measured every 2 h by UV-vis spectrophotometer. By comparison, the CQDs/CNS samples with different amounts of CQDs and original CNS was subjected to visible light irradiation, and the prepared CQDs/CNS photocatalyst as a dark control were also conducted. All the experiments were performed in triplicate.

The plate count method was selected to evaluate the bactericidal activity of the material. Firstly, E. coli (S.aureus) was cultured to the exponential growth phase, and bacterial pellets were obtained by low-speed centrifugation. After being diluted with phosphate buffer solution (PBS), CNS and CQDs/CNS were mixed with the diluted bacterial solution, respectively. After 30 min of the visible-light irradiation, the mixture was incubated in a 37 °C water bath shaker for 3 h. After that, 100 μL of the diluted solution was evenly coated on nutrient agar plates and then incubated in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h to evaluate the surviving colonies on the plate. The light control group without photocatalysts was conducted, and a series of experiments as the dark control group were carried out in the same conditions. Each sample was repeated in triplicate.

Typically, the bacterial suspension after light treatment was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. After removing supernatant, the collected bacterial cells were fixed with glutaraldehyde (2.5%) at 4 °C overnight, and then washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, the samples were dehydrated with 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90% and 100% ethanol solutions, respectively. Afterward, 10 μL of the suspension were dropped on clean silicon wafers, and dried naturally at room temperature. Finally, the morphology of E. coli (S.aureus) was observed separately using SEM. Each sample was measured in triplicate.
The SGC-7901 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium (containing 80 U·mL−1 penicillin, 0.08 mg·mL−1 streptomycin and 10% fetal bovine serum) at 5% CO2 atmosphere and 37 °C.

The logarithmic-phase SGC-7901 cells were cultured in 96-well plate with a suspension 100 μL cells (8 × 104 cells/mL) per well. The plates were incubated for 12 h at 37 °C with 100 μL of complete medium per well along with different concentrations of CQDs/CNS composites (0, 20, 40, 60, 80 μg·mL−1) for 24 h. 20 μL of Cell Counting Kit (CCK-8, Beyotime institute of biotechnology, Shanghai, China) was added to each well and the plates were further incubated for 2 h. The absorbance values of each well were measured by a microplate reader (BioTek Epoch, Vermont, USA) at 490 nm, and the cell viability was determined by the following formula:
Cell viability rate = (treatment group A490 nm/control group A490 nm) × 100%.

All data were analyzed using SPSS 18.0 software. Multiple mean comparisons were conducted using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). The difference between the two groups was analyzed using Dunnett’s t-test. Results were considered significant if p < 0.05.

The production of ROS was monitored through the degradation of 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA). The details as follow: the mixture of bacteria and CQDs/CNS (80 μg·mL−1) was incubated at 37 ℃ for 3 h. Then the bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was treated with DCFH-DA (100 mM) under visible light for 30 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was monitored on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000) with an excitation wavelength of 485 nm and an emission wavelength of 525 nm.

CNS and CQDs were synthesized based on previously reported works [35,36], and the CQDs/CNS samples with different CQDs mass were prepared by the hydrothermal methods. The specific diagram of synthetic process is illustrated in Scheme 1, and the details of synthesis are provided in Section 2.3. Due to the best bactericidal performance, the CQDs/CNS with the mass ratio of 1.5 wt% was characterized and applied in detail. For clarity, in the following text, “CQDs/CNS” refers specifically to “1.5 wt% CQDs/CNS” unless otherwise specified.

SEM and TEM were used to characterize the morphologies and microstructures of CNS and CQDs/CNS. The CNS exhibits a typical layered structure with irregular pores, which may be due to the gas released during the calcination process ( Fig. 1a) [37]. The CQDs/CNS displays a smooth-layered morphology with smaller pieces (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, the basic sheet of CQDs/CNS has more curves with ragged edges. In Figs. S2a and 2b, the CNS and CQDs/CNS all show lamellar structures, and nanosheets in the shape of veils can be observed in the CQDs/CNS, suggesting that the nanoplate can be well dispersed. Meanwhile, it can also be found that some CQDs with a diameter of 3 - 5 nm disperse well on the nanosheets (Fig. 1c). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1d) shows that the CQDs exhibit the intercrystalline (100) spacing of 0.21 nm, in consistent with previous reports [38]. With the excitation light changing from 350 nm to 450 nm, the strongest emission peak of CQDs shifts from 450 nm to 600 nm with the decrease in intensity, showing an excitation dependent down-transition behavior ( Fig. 2a) [39]. Notably, excited by 600 - 800 nm long-wavelength lights, the CQDs show an up-converted emission spectra ranging from 400 to 550 nm (Fig. 2b). The result shows that the CQDs can well transform visible or near-infrared light to short-wavelength light with higher photon energy [37]. More importantly, the up-converted effect of CQDs was inherited by the CQDs-CNS composites (Fig. S3). Thus, the CQDs act as a role of collecting light to enhance the utilization of visible light by the CNS, in favor of the improvement of photocatalytic activity [36].

Fig. 2c shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of the CNS and CQDs/CNS. The CNS and CQDs/CNS contain two diffraction peaks at 12.7° and 27.3°, corresponding to (100) and (002) lattice planes of g-C3N4, respectively, indicative of the stacking of aromatic periodic planar structure and graphite segment interface [40]. Moreover, the intensification of diffraction peak (002) in CQDs/CNS suggests that the CQDs contribute to the crystal layered structure of CNS [37]. In addition, no significant diffraction peak of CQDs is observed in the XRD spectrum of CQDs/CNS, which is mainly due to the low crystallinity or the low content of CQDs [41].

Fig. 2d displays the FT-IR spectrum of the samples. It can be clearly observed that the structure of g-C3N4 is unchanged after the introduction of CQDs. An obvious peak at 807 cm−1 in two samples attributes to the triazine ring mode [42]. The wide characteristic peaks at 3000 - 3700 cm−1 correspond to C-OH and N-H on the CNS surface, respectively [43]. Several peaks at 1200 - 1700 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of C-N heterocycles [44]. It is worth noting that, compared with the CNS, the intensity of peak of CQDs/CNS has a slight decrease in the band of 1400 - 1600 cm−1. This result suggests that the CQDs are well loaded on the CNS, and their strong interaction weakens the bond strength of C-N [45]. The finding is consistent with the results of XRD pattern, suggesting that the major triazine framework of the CNS is well maintained after the modification with CQDs. However, due to the low incorporated content of CQDs or the peaks of CQDs overlapped with that of CNS (Fig. S4), the FT-IR signals corresponding to CQDs was not labeled [46].

The chemical composition and structure of CNS and CQDs/CNS were tested by XPS (Fig. S5). In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s ( Fig. 3a), the peaks at 284.6 and 287.9 eV are assigned to CC bonds and sp2 carbon atoms bonded to N in an aromatic ring (N-CN), respectively[47]. And these two peaks maintain unchanged in the CQDs/CNS, suggesting that the structure of triazine rings is not affected by the introduction of CQDs. Particularly, a new peak at 285.1 eV (Fig. 3d), corresponding to C-OH in the CQDs/CNS, indicates that the hydroxyl-rich CQDs are coupled well with the CNS [48]. The N1s spectra (Fig. 3b, e) containing four peaks at 398.4, 399.4, 400.9, and 404.4 eV, can be assigned to sp2-bonded N in the triazine rings (C-N = C), tertiary N in N-(C)3, free amino groups (N-H) and graphitic species, respectively [49]. The O1s spectrum of CNS is consisted of two peaks at 531.1 and 532.2 eV, which is associated with C-O and OH/C-O-C, respectively (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3f shows a new peak at 533.5 eV, which belongs to OC-O in the CQDs/CNS, can be attributed to a significant increase in resident carboxyl groups caused by the CQDs [50]. These results indicate that the chemical framework structure of carbon nitride can be inherited in the CQDs/CNS.

Then, the porous properties and surface area were investigated. In Fig. 4a and b, the similar characteristic type-IV isotherm curves are observed, indicative of mesoporous structure in the pristine CNS and CQDs/CNS [51]. Moreover, the pore volume, and specific surface area of CQDs/CNS (0.55 cm3 /g, 67.6 m2 /g) are about twice as much as those of CNS (0.24 cm3 /g, 34.5 m2 /g), respectively. The introduced CQDs were anchored on the surface of CNS. Owing to the formation of CQDs/CNS interface, the surface roughness of CNS and the BET specific surface area of CQDs/CNS increase [51,52]. In general, the increased specific surface area can provide more active sites for the photocatalytic reaction, and the mesoporous architectures can provide more effective paths for the diffusion of reactants and products, both of which are conducive to the acceleration of catalytic reaction [51].

The ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded to investigate the optical absorption properties of CNS and CQDs/CNS. The intrinsic absorption edge of CNS and CQDs/CNS are found at 451 and 470 nm, respectively [53]. However, CQDs/CNS shows an obvious redshift, indicative of a stronger visible light response (Fig. 4c). The findings suggest that the CQDs can effectively improve the absorption capacity of CNS in the visible light [37,54]. Fig. 4d shows that the intrinsic bandgaps (Eg), which were calculated using the Kubelka-Munk method [54], are 2.63, and 2.55 eV for the CNS and CQDs/CNS, respectively. The bandgap of CNS is narrowed due to the combination with CQDs, which facilitates the transition of photogenerated carrier from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) and accelerates the formation rate of e--h+ pairs [55,56].

The determination of the band structure is of great significance in understanding the mechanism of photocatalysis. Fig. 5a and b shows the Mott-Schottky curves of CNS and CQD/CNS samples at different frequencies. The positive slopes indicate that they are n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potential (EF) of CNS and CQD/CNS relative to a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) were determined to be − 0.52 and − 0.63 V, respectively [57]. The band structures were further characterized by the XPS VB spectrum. As shown in Fig. 5c, the Ed values of the CNS and CQDs/CNS were estimated to be 1.87 and 2.02 eV, respectively. Thus, the VB/CB values of CNS and CQDs/CNS were calculated to be 1.35 eV/− 1.28 eV and 1.39 eV/− 1.16 eV, respectively, according to the formulas EVB - ECB = Eg and EVB – EF = Ed [33] (Fig. 5d). Obviously, the doping of CQD significantly changes the electronic structure.

The separation efficiency of photogenerated e--h+ pairs was analyzed by PL spectra. As shown in Fig. S6, both samples possess an absorption peak around 450 nm. Moreover, the emission peak intensity of CQDs/CNS is significantly lower than that of CNS. In general, lower fluorescence emission intensity is caused by lower e--h+ recombination rate, indicative of higher photocatalytic activity [58]. Moreover, the emission peak of CQDs/CNS shows a red shift, which is associated with the decrease in bandgap after CQDs modulation [59]. These results of optical and electrochemistry show that the CQDs play an important role in inhibiting the recombination of e--h+ pairs and promoting charge transfer, resulting in more charges participating in the photocatalytic reactions [60].

The transient photocurrent responses were recorded to investigate the charge generation and recombination behaviors in the CNS and CQDs/CNS photocatalysts. Notably, compared to the CNS, CQDs/CNS exhibits an approximately three-fold increase in photocurrent density (Fig. 5e), indicating that the introduction of CQDs is responsible for the faster charge transfer rate of photogenerated carriers [61]. Moreover, the good stability of CNS and CQDs/CNS photoelectrodes is confirmed by the repeatable photocurrent response during the "on-off" irradiation cycle. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) diagrams of the CNS and CQDs/CNS were illustrated in Fig. 5f. The Nyquist semicircle of CQDs/CNS exhibits a smaller radius than that of CNS, indicative of a lower charge transfer resistance of CQDs/CNS [62], further suggesting that the composite material CQDs/CNS possesses higher separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. These results also demonstrate that CQDs can promote the enhancement in photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4.

The growth trend of bacteria in the presence of the as-prepared photocatalysts was recorded at pH 7.4 to evaluate the antibacterial activity [63]. The CNS shows the MIC values of 200 and 300 ppm against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively ( Fig. 6a, g). By contrast, the MIC of 0.5 - 2.0 wt% CQDs/CNS against S. aureus decreases significantly to as low as 40 ppm. More outstandingly, the antibacterial activity against E. coli with a lower MIC of 12.5 ppm is superior to that of S. aureus (Fig. 6b - e, h - k), and even superior to the reported g-C3N4 based materials [30,64–66]. The MIC values of CNS and 0.5 - 2.0 wt% CQDs/CNS are tabulated in Table S1. For comparison, for the 1.5 wt% CQDs/CNS, no obvious bacteriostatic performance is observed in dark conditions (Fig. 6f, l).

Then, the plate counting method was carried out for evaluating bactericidal performance. As shown in Fig. 7a, when the concentration of the CQDs/CNS reaches 80 ppm, E. coli are almost inactivated in 30 min of visible light irradiation, whereas some colonies of S. aureus are still retained, in consistent with the results of MIC. The different bactericidal effect may be associated with the nature of bacteria. It is known that the gram-negative bacteria have a triple-layer cell wall with a thin peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane while the gram-positive bacteria exhibit a thicker peptidoglycan layer without an outer membrane [67]. For comparison, the bactericidal performance of CQDs (100 mg/mL) was independently explored. The bacterial number of E. coli as well as S. aureus is significantly unchanged when treated with CQDs in 30 min of visible light radiation (Fig. 7a), indicating that the efficient bactericidal performance is basically derived from the CNS. The bacteriostatic effect of CNS after hydrothermal treatment (denoted as CNS-O) was improved slightly, but the effect of CQDs/CNS composites was significantly stronger than that of CNS-O, which excludes the possibility that the hydrothermal treatment is the main reason for the promotion of performance. Thus, the enhanced inactivation effects is mainly due to the adding of CQDs which extends the light absorption to the visible light region [51]. However, the excessive CQDs can induce the aggregation of on the surface of CNS, thus reducing the separation efficiency of photo-generated e--h+ pairs [68], leading to the declination of bactericidal performance.

In order to evaluate the inactivation effects of CQDs, CNS, CNS-O, and CQDs/CNS against E. coli and S. aureus, the CFU quantification of bacterial inactivation were performed. In Fig. 7b, the inactivation of bacteria under the radiation of the visible light in 30 min were compared. The CFU reductions of E. coli in CQDs, CNS, CNS-O, and CQDs/CNS were 0.46, 6.91, 9.45, 33.02 × 107 CFU∙mL−1, respectively. The CFU reductions of S. aureus in the same four samples were 1.33, 8.19, 11.16, 36.51 × 108 CFU∙mL−1, respectively. Therefore, for the two bacteria in this work, the CFU reductions of four samples were as follow: CQDs/CNS > CNS-O > CNS > CQDs, which is consistent with the findings in previous plate counting method that the CQDs/CNS was more effective in inactivating E. coli and S. aureus.

SEM was carried out to observe morphological changes of bacteria before and after being incubated with CQDs/CNS. First, E. coli and S. aureus treated with CQDs/CNS were irradiated by the visible light for 30 min. Then, the collected bacterial cells were pretreated according to Section 2.8. Bacteria without CQDs/CNS were set as control group. As shown in Fig. 7c (①, ②) and 7d (①, ②), E. coli and S. aureus before treatment with CQDs/CNS show a typical rod-like structure and a round spherical structure with a smooth as well intact surface, respectively. However, after treatment by CQDs/CNS in 30 min of visible light radiation, the surface of these two bacteria becomes rough and wrinkled, or even ruptured in all directions (Fig. 7c (③, ④) and 7d (③, ④)). The shape changes of bacteria cells observed could be linked to the entering of viable but non culturable states (VBNC) where bacteria do not grow in liquid or solid media. However, these microorganisms are still viable and can undergo further recover [69].

The application of a specific antibacterial agent depends on the pharmaceutical effect as well as minimal toxicity [70]. Toxicity of CQDs/CNS was evaluated through the viability of SGC-7901 cells by CCK-8 cytotoxicity assay. Compared with the control group, the cell viability decreases insignificantly when the concentration of CQDs/CNS ranges from 20 to 80 μg·mL−1 (Fig. S7). However, the concentration of CQDs/CNS used is much less than 80 μg·mL−1, thus their cytotoxicity is negligible. These results show that the CQDs/CNS, as a potential antibacterial material, possesses an excellent biocompatibility.

The results of photoelectric properties show that the interaction between CQDs and CNS promotes the separation of photogenerated e--h+ pairs as well as electron transfer, which facilitates the production of ROS [71]. Then, a fluorescent probe (DCFH-DA) was used to evaluate the levels of ROS generated from CNS and CQDs/CNS under photocatalytic conditions [72,73]. As shown in Fig. S8, the ROS level of CQDs/CNS is significantly higher than that of the CNS treated group under visible light, implying that the interaction of CQDs with g-C3N4 significantly promotes the separation of photogenerated e--h+ and electron transfer properties, thereby increasing the level of ROS. These situations suggest that the increase of ROS generation may be the reason for the enhancement of antibacterial activity of CQDs/CNS. Furthermore, the 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) spin-trapping ESR technique was carried out to capture the ROS generated from CQDs/CNS under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8a, b, the pronounced ESR signals with a characteristic quadruplet of DMPO - •O2 - and DMPO - •OH adducts are clearly observed under visible light irradiation [45,74]. In comparison, ESR signal is silent in the dark, indicating •OH and •O2 - are served as active oxide species and are of great concern in the bactericidal activity.

Based on the aforementioned results, the enhanced photocatalytic activity under the visible light irradiation can be speculated by the formation of heterojunction between the CQDs and the CNS. The CNS could absorb light of wavelength < 500 nm under the visible light. Simultaneously, light of wavelength > 600 nm could be converted to < 550 nm light by up-conversion properties of the incorporated CQDs, i.e., absorbing low energetic photons and releasing high energetic photons [45,75], which could further widen the photo-response range, increase the utilization efficiency of solar energy, and excite the generation of e- - h+ pairs [45]. Furthermore, the relative band alignment provides the feasibility of photoexcited electrons migration from the CNS to the CQDs [76], thus facilitating charge separation and transfer at the junction interface.

Fig. 8c illustrates the proposed photocatalytic mechanism. It is clearly seen that the electrons on the CB can form •O2 - by a redox reaction [77]. However, the energy position of valence band maximum (VBM) of CQDs/CNS is 1.38 V (vs. NHE), which is lower than the standard redox potential of •OH/H2O (2.38 V vs. NHE) [78], thus •OH can't be a product of h+ on the VB reacting with water. In the CQDs/CNS system, the generation of •OH may be interpreted by equations [79]:

Therefore, the CQDs/CNS modulated by upconversion CQDs, on the one hand, extends the photo-response range to visible light and subsequently promotes the generation of e--h+ pairs. On the other hand, the shorter energy bandgap enhances the separation and transfer of charge at the junction interface. Alternatively, the e- reacts with O2 to form •O2 -, subsequently •O2 - reacts with h+ to generate •OH. In generally, the anion radical superoxide is not stable in water which would undergo disproportion reactions, leading to the formation of H2O2. The formed H2O2 would be easily reduced by CB electrons of CNS, producing the •OH radicals. Consequently, the most toxic ROS of •OH attacks the cell wall of E. coli (S. aureus), oxidizes such as lipopolysaccharide bilayer (LPS), phospholipid layer (PL), and peptidoglycan (PG) in bacteria cells, leading to the generation of aldehydes, ketones, and carboxylic acids [80]. This situation causes a detrimental damage to the bacterial cellular structure, eventually resulting in bacterial inactivation. However, in the dark conditions, negligible antibacterial results were found for both E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 7), which excluded the possibility of the direct contact between bacteria and CQDs/CNS [81].

In general, the excellent sustainability is a significant parameter to evaluate the usability of catalysts. The antibacterial performance of CQDs/CNS was repeated to investigate the reproducibility of the photocatalysis, wherein the recollected photocatalyst was centrifuged, filtered, dried, and used for the next run. As shown in Fig. 9a, the antibacterial efficiency is up to 80% after four cycles, indicative of an acceptable recyclability. The possible reason for the reduced bacterial inhibition may be that the ruptured bacteria adsorbed from the cell debris occupies the active site of the catalyst [82]. As indicated in Fig. S9, the leaching of CQDs or the loss of photocatalyst in the course of the cyclic experiments may be the other reason. Note that, no obvious changes of the diffraction peaks in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 9b) and XRD pattern (Fig. 9c) are observed, indicative of unaffected structure and crystallinity of the CQDs/CNS in the recycle.

In summary, a novel CQD@CNS nanocomposite with the visible light-triggered antibacterial activity has been successfully fabricated via a simple hydrothermal method. In the photocatalytic inactivation, CQDs acted as a photosensitizer to broaden the light absorption region, and simultaneously acted as an electron mediator to shorten the energy band gap. Compared with the CNS, the optimal photocatalyst (1.5 wt% CQDs/CNS) displayed superior photocatalytic activity for sterilization. Under the visible light radiation, the MIC against E. coli and S. aureus was as low as 12.5 and 40 ppm, respectively. It was demonstrated that the characteristic up-converted PL properties of the CQDs, which narrowed the energy bandgap, accelerated the electron separation and increased the ROS levels by effective charge transfer, was responsible for the drastically enhancement in photoactivity. This study provides a new perspective on the green catalysis that develops a CQDs-based g-C3N4 system for the effective bacterial disinfection.
